entries.def: A mnemonic for a variant response structure to the Roman Key Card Blackwood convention.
One round force.
A 2 or 3-level conventional opening bid made two steps below the opener's suit: for example, 2♦ to show a weak two bid in spades or 3♣ to show a three-level preempt in hearts.
A mnemonic for the original (Roman) response structure to the Roman Key Card Blackwood convention.
Fourth suit forcing.
Counting points by way of 8421 means counting an ace for 8 points, a king for 4, a queen for 2, and a jack for 1 point.

In rubber bridge, the location on the scorepad above the main horizontal line where extra points are entered; extra points are those awarded for holding honor cards in trumps, for bonuses for scoring game, small slam,…

American Contract Bridge League, the sport governing body for bridge in North America – defined as Bermuda, Canada, Mexico, and the United States – and the sponsoring organization of North American Bridge Championships…
An approach–forcing, natural bidding system, based on a weak NT and 4-card majors, popular in the United Kingdom and the Commonwealth.

In duplicate bridge, a score awarded by the Director (when empowered by the Laws) in order to redress damage to a non-offending side and to take away any advantage gained by an offending side through an infraction.
The cue bid of a first round control that occurs before a partnership has agreed on a strain.
A sacrifice bid made before the opponents have had an opportunity to determine their optimum contract.
Vulnerable against non-vulnerable opponents.
Deciding the outcome of a contest by totaling the raw points gained or lost on each deal.
For a partnership to come to a decision, explicitly, conventionally or by implication, on the denomination in which to play a hand.
An understanding between partners as to the meaning of a particular call or defensive play.
Declarer's intentional and unethical attempt to locate a finessable card by revoking.
A method of informing the opponents that partner's call carries a meaning they might not expect.
A method of promptly informing the opponents that partner's call has a particular meaning.
A call is antipositional if it tends to make the "wrong" partner the declarer.
In tournaments, to appeal is to request that a committee review a ruling made by a director.
A principle, first used in the Culbertson system, that has survived in modern bidding.
A marker, usually a large card with an arrow on it, that shows which direction is treated as North at a table in a duplicate event.
The action of changing the North direction during an event, typically for the last round of a Mitchell movement, so that the pairs who were North-South become East-West and vice versa.
A bid that, by prior agreement, requests information about a feature of partner's hand: for example, number of controls, suit length, or control of a particular suit.
A lead that instigates an active defense; often, the lead of an honor from a sequence, or a forcing defense.
Another name for Vienna System.
A variant of contract bridge for play by one person; alternatively, a means for one to learn or practice the game alone.
A squeeze position that succeeds against either opponent.
A play designed to keep a particular defender off lead, often to prevent the lead of a suit through a tenace position in either declarer's hand or dummy.
To make a partnership's first bid, having previously passed.
A combination of two finesses in a suit such that the first finesse is "backward": that is, leading away from the hand containing the tenace.
To keep the bidding open when it is about to be passed out at a low level.
A hand with balanced distribution in the narrow or wide sense just above.
Board-a-match, one method of scoring a duplicate bridge session or tournament.
To prevent a player from making a bid, either by a penalty caused by an irregularity, or because partnership agreement requires a pass in a given situation.
A bid which by partnership agreement requires partner to pass at future turns to call in the current auction.
In a duplicate event, the posting of contestants' running scores after each round.
A holdup by declarer, to prevent an opponent from continuing a suit.
In rubber bridge, the place on the score pad (below the main horizontal line) where trick points scored for making a contract, i.e. tricks bid for and taken exclusive of overtricks, are recorded.
A variant of Acol where 2♣ and 2♦ are strong bids of different strengths, and 2♥ and 2♠ are weak twos.
The trophy awarded to the winner of the World Zonal Open Team Championship, the most prestigious in bridge.
A commonly used term for the choice of minor suit opening bid with less than four cards, typically in five card major systems.
A bid erroneously made when it was another player's turn to bid.
A suit that a partnership regards as long and strong enough to be bid naturally.
A box placed on the table (one box for each player) that contains cards with calls printed on them, as well as other cards such as "alert".
The number of steps available in an auction, or the number of steps consumed by a bid.

(British slang) A sarcastic term applied to a poor result as a consequence of four steps: (1) the opponents are about to play in a part score, when you bid in pass-out seat, (2) the opponents then bid game, (3) you…
Break in tempo. See Tempo def 2.
Popular bidding convention in contract bridge, used to determine number of partner's aces/kings to evaluate for slam bids.
(Slang) A win by a sufficiently wide margin in IMPs to earn the maximum possible number (or difference) of victory points.
(Slang) A win by a sufficiently wide margin in IMPs to earn the maximum possible number (or difference) of victory points.
A form of scoring for teams, analogous to matchpoint scoring for pairs.
Intermediate cards such as the 9, 8 and 7, that contribute to a suit's trick-taking potential.

In bridge scoring, beyond points for bid tricks taken, which are awarded for making a contract, the additional points awarded for making a doubled contract, or for making doubled or redoubled overtricks.
(British slang) Adjective applied to a card found to be face-up during dealing, and by extension to the whole pack.
A group of entries in a tournament that will eventually have one winner.
A compilation of short "laws", "rules" and rules-of-thumb advice; often, not always, valid.
A sequence of honor cards, one or more of which is missing, for example AQJ.
(Slang) An ace.
A penalty double, in contrast to various competitive and informatory doubles including takeout doubles and negative doubles.
(Slang) A very weak hand. Sometimes paired with the name of a long suit: for example, "club bust" to denote a hand with long clubs and very little high card strength.
A method of scoring in duplicate bridge pairs events.
An assistant to the director, or Head Director, primarily responsible for moving boards between tables and collecting score slips.
An assistant to the director, or Head Director, primarily responsible for moving boards between tables and collecting score slips.
Any bid, pass, double, or redouble in the bidding stage.
An approach to bidding in which a player bids his shorter suit prior to his longer suit.
The act of determining the distribution of cards in unseen hands, and the location of high cards therein, by analyzing the bidding, play and other clues.
The act of determining the distribution of cards in unseen hands, and the location of high cards therein, by analyzing the bidding, play and other clues.
In a complex event, some participants begin a later stage with scores that depend on performance in an earlier stage.
A version of Chicago, with dealer's side not vulnerable on the second and third hands, as in the standard version.
Canadian Bridge Federation.
A bid in a new suit, as 1♠ in the sequence 1♦ – 1♥; 1♠.
A common conventional agreement following a 1NT rebid, searching for an unbid major suit or a preference to responder's major.
A variant of rubber bridge in which a rubber consists of four deals with vulnerability predetermined for each deal.
A hand without any trumps.
(Slang) Centre-hand opponent, a derogatory or facetious term for one's partner, or partners generally.
(Slang) An error in bidding or play, which was or might have been costly.
A suit with enough honor strength to play well unaided by partner's cards (but not solid) is chunky.
Knock out an opponent's high-card control of a suit, or unblock one's own high cards.
Declarer's hand (as distinct from the dummy, which is faced or open).
In a team match, a room where two of the pairs compete, and in which spectators are not allowed.
Making improper remarks to mislead the opponents, or asking improper questions designed to suggest a defensive play.
A contract that a player cannot fail to make, even against the best defense on any lie of the cards, is cold.
A bidding approach where players indicate suits (denominations) before showing high card strength.
A line of play that offers more than one chance to take additional tricks: for example, playing to drop an honor in a longer suit and then finessing for an honor in a shorter suit.
A line of play that offers more than one chance to take additional tricks: for example, playing to drop an honor in a longer suit and then finessing for an honor in a shorter suit.
A defensive signal that encourages partner to continue a suit, usually by means of the rank of the card used to follow suit.
A notrump overcall that shows a weak hand with a long suit, to which the overcaller can escape if doubled.
The method of scoring used in matchpoint or Board-a-Match events.
A statement by a player as to the number of remaining tricks that he must lose.
To act after an opponent's irregularity without arranging for the penalty specified in the Laws to be applied.
The unnecessary play (by follow-suit or by discard) of a jack following partner's exceptionally successful action.

(Mainly British) A nationally or locally organised duplicate bridge competition held at a single location and usually involving both pairs and teams events, typically lasting one or two days but sometimes as many as ten.
A bid that shows control of a particular suit.
A bid that shows control of a particular suit.
A bid that shows control of a particular suit.
See Short club.
A form filled out by a partnership and available to their opponents, that shows the bidding and play conventions they are using.
In the bidding, to choose (usually) partner's first bid suit; in that case, a correction is equivalent to a preference.
Acronym or initialism for Choice of Slams.
A defensive card play that shows whether the player has an even or odd number of cards in a suit.
The lead of a side suit in which both second and third hands are void, second hand holding a high trump, in such a way that third hand cannot be prevented from taking a trick with a low trump.
The lead of a side suit in which both second and third hands are void, second hand holding a high trump, in such a way that third hand cannot be prevented from taking a trick with a low trump.
See Scissors coup. "Coup without a name" is an earlier term for the coup, conferred by Ely Culbertson.
A card (honor or extra trump) which is known to compensate one of partner's losers; for example, a king in trumps covers partner's trump loser.
(Slang, verb). To make a penalty double.
On defense, second hand's play of a higher card than apparently necessary, so as to obtain the lead.
On defense, second hand's play of a higher card than apparently necessary, so as to obtain the lead.
To enter the opposite hand. Normally used of dummy or declarer's hand: "He crossed to dummy in diamonds."
A form of IMP scoring in pairs tournaments, where each pair's score is determined as an (averaged) sum of differences to all other scores (rather than to a single datum score).

A slam-seeking convention devised by Ely Culbertson, in which a player bids 4NT or 5NT to show possession of defined numbers of keycards (aces, and kings in bid suits), and to which that player's partner responds in…

A slam-seeking convention devised by Ely Culbertson, in which a player bids 4NT or 5NT to show possession of defined numbers of keycards (aces, and kings in bid suits), and to which that player's partner responds in…
The earliest dominant bidding system, developed by Ely and Josephine Culbertson.
The ♦9. The origin of the term is uncertain.
In rubber bridge, it is customary on completion of a rubber to invite other players in the cardroom to play in the next one, often by a cry of "Table up".
A form of three-handed bridge.
(Abbreviation of Declarative-Interrogative.)
An abbreviation of directional asking bid.
The mean or median of raw scores on a deal.
The practice of introducing an additional round (or rounds) to the bidding to show extra information.
The player who makes the first call in the auction.
The 52 cards used in bridge.
The contract in which a hand is played.
The side that wins the auction.
(Said of the contract). To prevent declarer from taking the number of tricks called for by his contract.
Declarer's opponents or their line of play.
The pair that tries to defeat the contract.
Postponed, as the jump preference in the auction 1♥ – 1♠; 2♦ – 3♥.
Postponed, as the jump preference in the auction 1♥ – 1♠; 2♦ – 3♥.
Acronym for Double Even, Pass Odd. Conventional method for bidding over interference with Blackwood.
On defense, the lead of an unsupported honor in order to create an entry to partner's hand.
The lowest spot card, the 2. In signaling, it is the only unambiguous card.
To establish tricks in a suit, usually by forcing out the opponents' stoppers.
In the endgame, the play of a side suit through a defender to create an over ruff and a subsequent trump finesse.
Usually said of a bid that is made immediately following RHO's bid.
A player's position at the bridge table (North, East, South or West).
Also tournament director (TD). The referee (in duplicate bridge).
A carding signal that discourages partner from leading a particular suit.
A play, either by declarer or by the defense, intended to obtain information about the location of other cards.
Acronym for Disturb Opponents Notrump. A conventional defense to notrump opening bids.
Acronym for Double Odd, Pass Even. A conventional method for bidding over interference with Blackwood.
A proxi-acronym for Double, O (the letter O standing for zero or none), Pass and I (the capital i standing for the numeral 1 or one).
(Adjective or adverb.) Said of a play or line of play that seems to be made with knowledge of all four hands, as if there were at least two dummies visible.

A bridge problem presented for entertainment or teaching, in which the solver is presented with all four hands and is asked to determine the course of play that will achieve or defeat a particular contract.
A finesse for two missing cards.
To double a part score such that, if the contract is fulfilled, the total of the doubled trick scores will exceed 100 points.
A form of knockout competition in which teams are eliminated after losing two matches rather than after losing one.
An agreement regarding a second negative bid by a player who has already made one.
A raise of two levels, such as 1♠ – 3♠.
A squeeze in which each opponent must guard a different suit, and both opponents must guard a third suit.
Double knockout.
To bid the higher of two adjacent suits before the lower.
To extract, usually trumps. To remove the opponents' trump cards is to "draw trumps."
To force a stopper from an opponent's hand, usually by repeatedly leading the suit.
A play technique in which a player does not immediately play a card that might take a trick, but plays a small card instead.
The play of the hand by declarer. The apparent contradiction is due to the fact that declarer plays both declarer's cards and the dummy's.
A playing technique in trump contracts that gains extra tricks by ruffing in the hand that began with the longer trumps so that that hand ends up with shorter trumps.
To lose a match deliberately, usually so as to assist another team or pair in the event.
Possession of values in the same suit in both partners' hands so arranged that they do not pull their full weight.
A bidding response of 1NT to an opening bid that doesn't show a balanced hand but a weak hand (6–9 HCP), no support for partner and no higher ranking 4+ card suit to bid.
A bidding style that developed in the Eastern United States, particularly the New York region.
(Slang, chiefly British) A hand with 7–4–1–1 distribution, from the cologne brand 4711.
European Bridge League, the sport governing body for contract bridge in Europe, and the sponsoring organisation for many bridge competitions there.
English Bridge Union, the official organising body of bridge in England.

The play of first the higher, then the lower of two cards of the same suit on separate tricks to encourage or, by prior agreement, to discourage (see upside-down signals) partner's continuation of a suit; or to signal…
Every Hand An Adventure, a bidding style that emphasizes very weak notrump opening bids (often 10–12 HCP), four-card majors, and undisciplined weak-two bids.
Exclusion Keycard Blackwood, a variant of Roman Keycard Blackwood.
The removal, by playing a suit or suits, of safe exit cards from defenders' hands, normally in preparation for an endplay.
To win a trick by ruffing with a trump lower in rank than an opponent's trump.
An agreement that the meaning of bids or card signals may change as more information about a deal becomes available.
The layout of the cards when just a few tricks remain to be played.
A play which forces a particular opponent to win a trick, so that that opponent must then make a favorable lead.
A squeeze in which the declarer decides whether to overtake the squeeze card or to let it hold the trick, depending on the play of the intervening opponent.
A squeeze that puts pressure on a holding that interferes with declarer's entries.
A squeeze in which the declarer decides whether to overtake the squeeze card or to let it hold the trick, depending on the play of the intervening opponent.
An agreement concerning rebids after take-out doubles.
Cards in one hand that are adjacent in rank and thus have equal trick-taking power.
A long suit to which a bidder can escape if necessary or desirable.
A duplicate bridge contest.
A duplicate bridge contest.
A bid, such as 2♦ in the Roman Club system, that shows length in all suits except the one named.
An agreement that responder to a Blackwood bid will show the number of aces held outside a particular suit.
A card that is used to put a different hand on lead, normally to avoid making a self-destructive lead in another suit.
Someone who plays bridge better than others in their usual level of play.
A card whose suit and rank become known through an irregularity.
Values (in the form of High card points, shortage or cover cards), which are in addition to the values that a player has promised so far in the bidding.
Forcing one round. See One round force.
A king, queen, or jack. Contrast Honor.
A king, queen, or jack. Contrast Honor.
The adjustment of matchpoint scores to correct for dissimilar conditions.
To be captured by a higher card. See drop.
A return to partner's first-bid suit despite a longer holding in the second suit.
Phantom sacrifice.
A card played with the intention of deceiving an opponent as to one's true holding.
A style of bidding under which the fewer bids used to reach a contract (usually said of game contracts), the weaker the bidder's hand.
A rubber completed in two games. See slow rubber.
An honor or shortness in a suit. Conventional bids such as splinter bids or D-I are intended to show or elicit features.
(Slang) Short for "fertilizer", a very weak opening bid.
an abbreviation for forcing to game; see Game force
Deciding correctly that partner has psyched in the absence of a call that reveals the psych.
Mid-rank cards that strengthen a suit. See body.
The last bid made on a hand.
A bid in a suit that shows length and strength in the bid suit plus a fit for partner's suit.
An obsolete term for making a preemptive bid.
An obsolete term for making a preemptive bid.
A conventional opening bid of two diamonds (some prefer two hearts instead) to show 11–15 HCP with 5 hearts and 4 spades.
An adaptation of the Howell movement in which the players, rather than the boards, progress regularly from table to table.
An adaptation of the Howell movement in which the players, rather than the boards, progress regularly from table to table.
An adaptation of the Howell movement in which the players, rather than the boards, progress regularly from table to table.
To play a card of the same suit as the one that was first led to the trick.
To bid with the intention of causing the bidding to proceed to a particular level.
The lead and subsequent continuation of a suit that the defenders believe declarer will have to ruff in the long trump hand.
An agreement that a 1NT response to a 1♥ or 1♠ opening is a forcing bid.
Obsolete name for a strong jump shift by responder.
Obsolete name for a strong jump shift by responder.
A tenace.
A board whose cards are not distributed as they were when first played, due to returning the cards to their slots erroneously.
An agreement that an opening bid of 1♥ or 1♠ promises at least four cards in the suit bid.
See Chicago.
The fourth player with an opportunity to bid, or to play to a trick.
The fourth player with an opportunity to bid, or to play to a trick.
A holding of three or even two cards in a suit, thus not long enough to suggest as a trump suit.
A holding of three or even two cards in a suit, thus not long enough to suggest as a trump suit.
A second-round jump bid (usually a double jump) that by agreement shows a fit with partner's last-bid suit and shortness in another suit.
A hand with a very long suit or suits. Most would regard a hand with two six card suits as a freak.
A bid that is made when a pass would still allow partner to make a bid.
A position in which a player leads up to an opponent's tenace, solving that opponent's possible guess.
A frozen suit is one that neither side can play without damage to its own holding in the suit.
An opening bid of 3NT. The bidder hopes to make the contract by means of a long minor suit rather than by a preponderance of high cards.
A bid that asks partner not to pass before the partnership's bidding has reached game (or the opponents have been doubled at a level high enough to compensate).
Said of a card or cards that have been established.
A bidding system dominant in the United States from the 1940s through the 1960s, based on the Culbertson system.
A style of dealing, usually in rubber and Chicago games, where the cards are not thoroughly shuffled between deals and are dealt in groups.
A trump coup in which the cards ruffed in the long trump hand are already winners.
A method of determining whether the partnership holds the top trump honors when the bid of a grand slam is a possibility.
(Slang, mainly British) Non-vulnerable. From the colour of the paint on a duplicate board.
A play that creates no direct advantage and might lose.
A holding that prevents an opponent from taking a trick or tricks.
See distribution.
See distribution.
A document that lists the cards in each hand of every board played in a duplicate bridge session.
The bid of a side suit after a single raise, used to help partner evaluate game prospects when opener's hand is roughly a trick stronger than a minimum opening.
Use of the cheapest bid (sometimes only the cheapest suit bid) as an artificial negative response to (for example) forcing 2-bids, strong artificial 2♣, or takeout doubles.
A brief pause before a bid or play, considered somewhat shorter than a Huddle.
A measure or estimate of the strength of cards in the play of a deal.
See Echo.
An ace, king, queen, jack or ten.
An ace, king, queen, jack or ten.
A method of hand evaluation used in the Culbertson system, which assigns point values to honors and combinations of honors.
An ace, king, queen, jack or ten.
At rubber bridge and Chicago, a scoring bonus.
An employee of a bridge club who is available as a fourth.
A pairs tournament movement where each pair typically plays against all or most of the other pairs, and there is a single set of winners.
Acronym or initialism for Highly Unusual Methods.
Acronym or initialism for Highly Unusual Methods.
(Said of a card) Available as a discard; not required for purposes such as guarding the opponents' suit or interfering with their communications.
A breach of ethical conduct or etiquette; an action that violates the proprieties.
The form of duplicate bridge that uses International Match Points as a scoring method, as distinct from a tournament scored using matchpoints (MPs).
♠ Q 5 4 Irrelevant W N↑ S↓ E ♠ K 7 6 ♠ A 9 8
♠ Q 5 4 ♠ K 7 6 W N↑ S↓ E Irrelevant ♠ A 9 8
A form of duplicate bridge, scored at matchpoints, in which each player is paired with a different partner on each round.
A double that is intended to convey information rather than to exact a penalty from the opponents.
A player’s breach of the Laws of Bridge or of a lawful regulation made under them.
A bid that is not higher than the immediately preceding bid, and is therefore illegal.
(Slang) The bonus for making a doubled or redoubled contract is sometimes referred to as the "insult" or as being "for the insult".
A bid, usually a sacrifice bid, intended to keep the opponents from playing their presumed or inferred optimum contract.
A call, such as an overcall or an initial preempt, that is intended to make it more difficult for the opponents to bid to their best contract.
1) Nines, eights and sevens are sometimes termed "intermediate cards."
The first player on the other side to make a call other than pass when one side has opened the bidding.
A technique that involves successive finesses against both opponents.
An agreement that treats the single raise of a minor suit as strong, and a double raise as preemptive.
International Popular Bridge Monthly, a British bridge magazine.
A hackneyed phrase that describes the play of a high card by a player whose high card holding is led through; or, that player's statement.
A breach of procedure, as described in the Laws and Proprieties, in bidding or play.
(Said of a menace card) To isolate a menace in squeeze play is to arrange that only one opponent can guard one of declarer's threat suits.
By agreement, a forcing raise of a major suit opening bid, used in conjunction with limit jump raises.

A bidding convention initiated by responder following partner's notrump opening bid that requests opener rebid in the suit ranked just above that bid by responder, i.e. a response in diamonds requests a rebid in hearts…
(Slang) To preempt.
The discard of an honor, often by a defender, and usually to unblock a suit.
Grand slam force, an alternative term popular in Europe.
Opening lead convention, mainly against notrump contracts, designed to show both what the leader has, and to request specific partner actions in return.
An overcall made at higher than the minimally legal level: for example, 1♥ – (2♠).
A preference to partner's first-bid suit, made at a level higher than the minimally legal level.
A raise of partner's suit one level higher than the minimum legal raise.
A rebid of one's original suit, one level higher than necessary, usually showing a six-card suit: for example, 1♦ – 1♥; 3♦.
A jump bid of a new suit.
A player under the age of 26. Various national, regional, and world competitions use this designation.
A bidding system that uses five card majors and the weak notrump.

entries.term: Keycard Blackwood, or Key Card Blackwood (KCB)
entries.def: A variant of the Blackwood convention in which five keycards are counted, four aces plus the king of the apparent trump suit, rather than four aces alone.
A spectator who attends a game in person.
An ace-asking or keycard-asking convention initiated by the first step above four of the apparent trump suit rather than uniformly by 4NT.
At pairs, plus or minus 200. A score of minus 200, down two undoubled and vulnerable, or down one doubled and vulnerable, is a likely bottom against a part score by the opponents.
A single-elimination tournament for teams-of-four.
A type of squeeze that operates in part against the defender's trump holding, when the defender threatens to win a plain suit trick and then lead a trump, thus reducing declarer's ruffing tricks.
A rescue mechanism employed when partner's bid is doubled for penalties.
A conventional bid that is one step above the current bid and one step below game in a trump suit.
A board that is played after the remainder of the event has finished, usually because of slow play or an irregularity.

entries.term: Law of Total Tricks (LTT), or "The Law"
entries.def: A guideline stating that the total number of cards held by both sides in their longest trump fits equals the total number of tricks available to both sides in their best trump contracts.

entries.term: Laws of Contract Bridge and Laws of Duplicate Bridge
entries.def: The definitions, procedures and remedies that define how rubber bridge and duplicate bridge are played.
A contract that can be made on any rational line of play.
Playing a card when it was another player's turn to lead.
A maxim that advises a defender to lead a suit in which LHO has high card strength, forcing declarer to play high or low before third hand plays.
A double by the partner of the prospective opening leader that requests the lead of a particular suit.
To make a jump bid.
A conventional overcall in 4♣ or 4♦ made in defense to opposing 2-level or 3-level preemptive openings.
To pass, often used of passing when partner's double was followed by a pass.
Responder's bid of 2NT as a puppet to 3♣ in preparation for a sign-off.
(Slang) game. Normally used in reference to rubber bridge.
The number of cards held in a suit.
(Slang) To allow a contract to make by misdefense.
(Slang, verb) To double. (In Britain at least, a penalty double: Leave 'er in.
(Adv.) To enter the auction with relatively low values (for example, to "open light" or "overcall light").
A penalty double, usually of a slam contract, that requests partner to choose an unusual suit for the opening lead.
In the bidding, to define a hand's strength with some degree of precision.
A bid which establishes narrow limits on both the high card strength and distribution of the bidder's hand.
An invitational jump raise of a major suit, such as 1♥ – 3♥.
1) (with "the"): A line on a bridge scorepad that separates points for tricks that count toward game (see Below the line) from those that do not (see Above the line).
1) (Noun) A contract that is certain to succeed.
Little Old Lady (pronounced El-Oh-El). A facetious reference to a seemingly weak player.
Cards of the same suit, remaining in one hand, after all the other cards in that suit have been played from the other hands.
In a partnership, the hand with the longer trumps.
Following a major suit raise to the two level, the long suit game try names a suit with at least four cards, so that partner's cover card is useful regardless of length in the suit.
A card play tactic that attempts to create an advantage by playing two losers, often of different suits, on the same trick.
A method of hand evaluation based on counting losers.
No score. "Love all" means that neither side is vulnerable.
(Adjective) A card that is not expected to take a trick.
On defense, to play a higher card, having already played a lower one, so as to convey information to partner.
Limit Raise Or Better. Example: "1H-2NT = Heart-support, LROB."
A synonym or close variant of the Muiderberg convention, a weak two-bid showing 5 cards in a major and at least four cards in another suit.
A defensive card that, if retained, is a liability on one line of play, but that, if played, will be missed on another line of play.
A card that is exposed by a defender prematurely and through intentional play; or, an honor card that is exposed prematurely even if accidentally.
The highest and the third highest remaining cards in a suit, held in the same hand.
(Verb) To take at least as many tricks as a contract calls for.
(Adjective) An unsophisticated game, approach to bidding, or line of play.
(Noun) A type of relay bid in which the cheapest response is expected nearly all the time, thus similar but not identical to a puppet bid.
To be known to hold a particular card: "He was marked with the ♥Q."
A finesse for a card that evidently lies with a particular opponent.
The highest card of a suit that is yet to be played.
Units awarded, usually by national organisations, for successful performance in a bridge tournament.
A series of hands played by two teams in knockout events.
The layout of the cards that play pivotal roles in certain endplays, most typically squeezes.
By prior agreement, a game-invitational double of an overcall that leaves no room for a bid, when a bid would invite game.
A maxim of bridge is a brief expression of a general principle of the game.
See Suit preference signal.
A card that requires an opponent to retain a higher card in the same suit, as a guard.
The deliberate sacrifice of a high card to remove a vital entry to an opponent's hand, usually the dummy.
By prior agreement, an immediate cue bid in the suit of an opponent's opening bid, such as 1♦ – (2♦), for two-suited takeout.
A simplified form of contract bridge designed to expose newcomers to declarer and defensive playing techniques without the burden of learning a detailed bridge bidding system.
A card below the rank of an honor card that is exposed by a defender prematurely but accidentally, via mishap.
The second-highest and the fourth-highest (or lower) remaining cards in a suit, held in the same hand.
Identical hand distributions: "North and South had mirror distributions."
A bid that fails to describe the hand properly.
Two partnership hands, neither of which can support the other's long suit.

A pairs tournament movement in which the pairs sitting in one direction (usually North-South) stay in the same seats throughout, but after each round the pairs sitting in the other direction (usually East-West) move to…
1) Of an event: contested by pairs or teams in which every pair comprises one male and one female player.
A play that forces the defense to choose between taking a high card that will establish extra winners for declarer, and ducking the trick, after which the high card cannot be cashed.

In a tournament, the scheme for the progression of players and boards from table to table, arranged so that a pair does not play the same boards twice, or meet the same opponents twice etc. The most common movements for…
A 4–3 trump fit, or a contract with such a trump fit.
Acronym of "Middle, Up, Down", a lead convention which describes the sequence in which cards from a holding of three low ones (all less in rank than the 10) are played.
A weak two-bid showing five cards in a major and at least four cards in another suit.
An ambiguous opening bid of 2♦ that promises one of several different types of hand.

A call which indicates either: (1) a willingness to play the contract named, (2) a suit bid suggesting length or strength in that suit, (3) a notrump bid that suggests a balanced hand, (4) a double that suggests the…
Nederlandse Bridge Bond (Dutch Bridge League).
A conventional call used by responder in a competitive auction to denote possession of at least one unbid suit.
Responder's suit bid following an opening bid and an overcall.
An inference based on something that did not happen.
A bid that shows insufficient values for a stronger response.
In a competitive auction, the double of a voluntarily bid slam to show no defensive tricks, and therefore to suggest a sacrifice.
In duplicate pairs tournaments, a method of fairly adjusting match point scores when not all boards have been played the same number of times.
By agreement, after 1m – 1M; 1NT, a bid of two of the unbid minor as artificial and forcing, often requesting three card support for responder's bid major or four cards in the unbid major.
A suit that has not yet been bid.
New minor forcing.
An alternative to "pass". Used in the United Kingdom, where "pass" might be mis-heard as "hearts."
A suit which has not yet been bid by either opponent.
In rubber bridge, the state of the score in which neither pair has made a game.
A bid which partner may pass. See also Forcing bid, Invitation, Sign-off.
A slam-seeking convention
One of the partnerships designated on duplicate boards.
The state of vulnerability in which both bonuses and penalties are smaller.

entries.term: Notrump distribution, no trump distribution, or NT distribution
entries.def: Balanced distribution. WikiProject Contract bridge deprecates the two-word "No Trump".
A contract, or a bid that names a contract without a trump suit.
Non-playing captain.
An interference bid whose principal aim is not to preempt or to compete for the contract, but nevertheless to upset the smooth flow of the opponents' bidding sequence.
A very large penalty: "He went for a number."
1) Of a finesse: A duck, made in the hope that a high card will fall.
Specifying a level. To make 4♥ is to make four-odd.
The number of tricks above six (the book) that are taken by declarer.
A defensive carding scheme under which the play of an odd-numbered card is encouraging and that of an even-numbered card is discouraging.
1) (Slang) Down, or set. "We're off two" means "We have made two fewer tricks than our contract."
Having a distribution that does not quite conform to that suggested by a bid, such as an opening bid of 1NT with 2=2=6=3 shape, or a weak-two bid with a seven card suit.
Said of some number of tricks that can be lost or won without gaining or losing the lead.
High ODR means a hand has characteristics more suited to winning the final contract, while Low ODR means it has characteristics more suited to defending against opponents' contract.
Unfavorably located, from the point of view of the player taking a finesse.
A world bridge championship held every four years under the auspices of the World Bridge Federation.
1) Makeable. A contract that can be made is said to be on.
A bidding system that uses a bid of 1♣ as artificial and forcing, but not necessarily strong.
To an opening one-bid, any one-level response in a suit; that is, one of a higher suit in response to opening one of a lower suit.
A bid that requests partner to ensure that the bidding continue for at least one more round.
A hand with only one long suit, normally refers to a hand with a six card or longer suit.
Favorably located, from the point of view of the player taking a finesse.
Opener's second bid.
The declarer's LHO, who always makes the opening lead.
A member of the other partnership or team.
In unopposed bidding, the contract that cannot be improved upon by further bidding, nor could have been improved upon by taking a different line in earlier bidding.
(Informal) A hand on which "our" side can take more tricks than their side.
See Advance cue bid.
See In back of.
1) (Noun) A bid that overstates a hand's strength.
(Slang) Having overbid.
The player making an overcall; may also be referred to as the intervenor.
To play a card higher than the winning card played by partner, unnecessary to win the trick but necessary to gain the lead.
Deck of cards.
Two players playing bridge together as partners.
A form of duplicate bridge in which each pair competes separately, as distinct from team and individual events.
(Slang) Someone who plays bridge worse than others in their usual level of play
A defence to a Strong Club whereby two-level bids show the suit bid or the other three suits.
A competition that uses composed deals, designed to test each pair's bidding and its card play.
That contract which results from optimal bidding by both sides, and which neither side could improve by further bidding.
On a given deal, the score that results from best bidding and best play by both sides.
(Slang) Partner.
1) A trick score less than 100, obtained by making a contract.
An endplay in which declarer is unable to remove all possible safe defensive exit cards, and must hope that the remaining cards are so distributed that the defense cannot get off lead safely.
The other member of the partnership.
1) See pair.
Sequences in which the opponents do not compete.
A service, provided by some tournaments, that locates a partner for a player who does not yet have one.
An agreement between partners, reached prior to the beginning of play, concerning the meaning of a call or of carding.
To make a forcing pass and on the next round remove partner's double by bidding.
A bid made in response to partner's ambiguous call.
A player who passed instead of opening the bidding.
An approach to defending a hand that emphasizes waiting for tricks that declarer must eventually lose, getting off lead safely, and avoiding plays that will set up tricks for declarer.
See distribution.
High card points plus number of spades held.
1) A score awarded to the defense when declarer's contract goes down.
A card, incorrectly exposed by the defense, whose subsequent proper play is governed by certain rules.
The pass of an informatory double, to convert it to a penalty double.
In matchpoint scoring, refers to the number of matchpoints actually scored by a pair on a board, session, or event, as a percentage of the maximum number available.
A play that is chosen because the mathematics of suit distribution suggests that it is more likely to succeed than an alternative line.
A record of the board number, opposing pair number, contract, declarer, tricks taken, and raw score kept by each player for the boards played by the partnership in a single session.
(Slang; chiefly British) See Echo. The term is said to derive from the Blue Peter, a nautical signal.
In a pairs movement, if there is an odd number of pairs, then in each round one pair will have to sit out.
A sacrifice bid against a contract that the opponents would not have made.

A type of 1 Club opening bid which shows opening values but does not guarantee clubs, denies a five card major (and often 5 diamonds as well) and may have as little as one club (on a 4441 shape hand).
(Slang) A hand that is so easy it plays itself.
1) (Verb) To run a suit without losing a trick in it.
A type of score slip on which the result of a deal is recorded for the purpose of comparative scoring.
The lead of a high card from one hand to capture a singleton of lower rank in an opponent's hand.
1) A spot card.
To discard.
1) (Adjective) Of the suit that both defenders must guard in a double squeeze.
A French, whist-like card game whose scoring foreshadowed that used in contract bridge.
A suit that is not trump; a side suit.
1) (Noun) The stage of a deal when players attempt to take tricks.
To assume that the opponents have a particular distribution or holding, and to plan and conduct the play on that basis.
1) (Of a contract) A rational, if not necessarily optimal, choice of strain and level.
Cards, such as long cards, that will take tricks (usually, for declarer), and that therefore contribute to a hand's strength.
Acronym or initialism for Petty Little Odious Bid, another name for New Minor Forcing.
One of four slots in a duplicate board that hold the cards between plays.
A proxi-acronym for Pass=0, Double=1. Method for countering interference over Blackwood.
1) A scoring unit: e.g., a trick taken by declarer in a minor suit contract scores 20 points.
A method of hand evaluation which assigns a numeric value to a hand's high cards and distributional features, used as a guideline in bidding.
A hand whose intrinsic trick-taking potential is less than a conventional point count would indicate.
Another name for board-a-match.
Spades or diamonds. The term refers to the shape at the tops of the suit symbols.
A bridge club in London which published the first version of the Laws of contract bridge.
(Noun) Seat at the table: North, South, East, West; or first, second, third, fourth.
A squeeze that can succeed against only a specific opponent, because at least one threat must lie over its guard.
A bid that announces the possession of at least minimum values.
(Slang) A discussion of a hand, and the nature of the result, after the play has concluded.
An unusually strong hand.
An alert which must be made at the beginning of the round before play begins on the first board.
A bidding system that combines the features of Kaplan–Sheinwold with a strong, artificial 1♣ opening bid.

entries.term: Preempt, preemptive bid, or preemptive raise
entries.def: 1) A bid (or raise) predicated on length of a suit rather than overall strength, primary function of which is to interfere with the opponents' bidding by taking away bidding space they need to exchange information.
A call that returns the bidding to partner's first-bid suit; for example, in 1♥ – 1♠; 2♦ – 2♥, 2♥ is a preference.
A bid which differs from usual partnership practice that is chosen to avoid a later bidding problem.
See short club.
A carding agreement under which a count signal shows the number of cards currently held.
A guideline to the play of the hand, concerning the probability of the location of key cards in the unseen hands.
The movement of players and deals between rounds in an event.
A squeeze in three suits that, when it matures, results in a new squeezed position in two suits.
1) In the play, to cause a card to become a winner.

A section of the Laws of Duplicate Contract Bridge that describes, in general terms, proper conduct as to the exchange of information concerning a hand, as to attitude and etiquette, as to partnership agreements, and as…
See balance. (In the UK, protect is the more usual term.)
See appeal. (In the UK, protest is the more usual term.)
A position that, to a defender, appears to be a true squeezed position, but is not.

entries.term: Psych, psyche, psychic, or psychic bid
entries.def: A call that grossly misstates high card strength or distribution, made so as to deceive the opponents.
A bid that, by partnership agreement, announces that the player's previous bid was a psychic.
1) To remove the opponents' trumps.
(slang) To force out an opponent's trump, usually by means of a forcing defense.
An artificial bid that simply requests partner to make a specified cheap reply – commonly the cheapest sufficient bid, or next step.
A version of Stayman employed after an opening bid of 1NT or 2NT which could include a five-card major.
1) (Verb) To force the opponents to make any subsequent call at a level higher than they have as yet.
A portmanteau of queen and jack. Used in situations where it does not matter whether the queen or the jack is held or played, as well as to emphasize that it does not matter.
(Adjective) A session or sessions preliminary to the final of an event.
1) Of a bid: A call based, usually, on high card points, rather than a feature such as fit or shortness.
In Key Card Blackwood, the cheapest bid over the response to 4NT, to ask responder for the trump queen.
In card play, top ranking holdings able to win tricks immediately.
A trick whose cards have all been turned face down (duplicate bridge) or gathered in front of the trick's winner (rubber bridge).
Points won divided by the sum of points won and points lost, occasionally used to break a tie.
A movement used in individual events.
A trick consisting of all four suits, typically involving low cards.
A card that enables a hand to gain the lead on a later trick, after that hand has already gained the lead with a different entry card.
A suit with sufficient length and strength, according to partnership agreements, to be rebid in certain defined circumstances.
(Abbreviation of "recapitulation") A summary of results in a bridge tournament.
A member of a bridge organization whose responsibility it is to maintain a record of reports of possible violations of the Proprieties.
To lose some number of tricks in preparation for a squeeze.
(Slang) Vulnerable. From the color of the paint on a duplicate board.
In rubber bridge, the prescribed remedy for a faulty deal.
(Verb). Of a trick, to duck.
To fail to comply with a bid that has made a request, such as an invitation or a transfer.
An artificial bid that requests partner to further describe his hand.
A bidding system that consists of many relay bid sequences.
(Slang) Redouble; by extension from lever.
To bid on over an undesired contract, especially a doubled contract.
See balance.
A form of knockout competition in which winners advance and losers drop into a secondary event from which some number of top performers return to the primary event. [clarification needed]
To remove from a contract that partner has bid and which, often, has been doubled.
A double that follows LHO's opening bid, partner's takeout double and RHO's raise of opener's suit, to show moderate values and no clear opinion as to the best strain.
(Slang) One who evaluates bids and plays according to their outcome, rather than to their intrinsic merit.
Maintain the right to lead to the next trick by leading and winning the current trick.
To lead back, usually the suit that partner led.
A bidding sequence in which a single player, on consecutive calls, bids two different suits, and bids the two suits in the reverse order to that expected by the basic bidding system.
(Slang) To redouble.
A style of bidding which strives to select the Declarer most beneficial for the partnership.
To play a high card in the hope of taking a trick: "Rise with the ace."
Roman Key Card Blackwood, a slam bidding convention.
A rare end position which combines elements of ruff and discard, trump shortening, and endplaying to force an opponent to concede a trump trick
Descriptive of bids and carding agreements used or originated in the Roman system:
Acronym for "Raise (is the) Only Non-Force".
The award for winning the world knockout team championship that is held in even numbered years other than leap years.
The progression of the bidding and play in a clockwise direction around the table.
A bidding system popular in the U.S. during the 1960s.
An event format in which each team eventually opposes each other team.
Hearts or clubs. The term refers to the shape at the tops of the suit symbols.
In rubber bridge, the set of successive deals that ends when one of the pairs wins two games.
A bonus awarded to the pair winning the rubber: 500 points if the losers are vulnerable, 700 if they are not.
The original form of contract bridge, a contest with four players in two opposing pairs (as distinct from duplicate bridge, which requires a minimum of eight players).
Transfer advances of overcalls. See Useful Space Principle.
The lead of a suit in which both opponents are void, so that one opponent can ruff while the other discards (or sluffs).
To establish a suit by ruffing one or more of its low cards.
Regulation by the World Bridge Federation stipulating that an opening bid is acceptable if the sum of the number of cards in the two longest suits plus the number of HCP is at least 18.
A calculation that can be used when it is assumed that opening leader has led the fourth highest card in a suit.
Guideline for opening light in fourth seat: open if your high card points plus your number of spades is 15 or more.
When the bidding has reached the 5-level in a competitive auction, tend to defend rather than bid on.
Avoid giving support for partner's 5-card suit if a superior 4–4 fit might be available.
When declarer's only high card in the suit led by the opponents is the ace, count the number of cards in that suit held by declarer and dummy, subtract from seven and duck that many times.

On a competitive part score deal, with the points roughly equal between your side and theirs, once the bidding has reached the 3-level, tend to defend rather than bid on (unless your side has 9 trumps).

A widely used guideline of the Standard American Yellow Card (SAYC) bidding system which states that a hand may open bidding "normally" (that is, by bidding one of a suit) if the sum obtained by adding the combined…
When missing two non-touching honors, it is normally superior to finesse first for their lower honor.
A bidding guide suggested by Ely Culbertson, which counsels preemptors to be within two tricks of their contract if vulnerable, and within three if not.
A finding and decision by a tournament director or appeals committee.
To play the winners in a suit.
An agreement to lead the second highest of touching honors.
(Slang) Sacrifice. Also, "sack."
A level at which the partnership can normally assume, on the basis of the previous bidding, that its contract will succeed.
A play that maximizes the chances for fulfilling the contract (or for achieving a certain score) by avoiding a play which might result in a higher score.
(Slang) To bid weakly or pass with good values, in the hope that the opponents will get overboard.
An overcall made after an opening bid and response by the opponents.
(Slang) Sacrifice.
A style of bidding that attempts to narrowly limit the strength of a partnership's hands, so as to make its bidding more accurate.
A loser-on-loser play meant to break the opponents' communications.
A paper form used to record the result of each deal in a duplicate bridge event when electronic scoring devices are not available.
1) The numeric result of a deal, session or event.
1) To bid to a safer contract.
A device which divides the table diagonally, visually separating partners from each other.
Opponents who sit on the same side of the screen.
Position relative to the dealer: for example, dealer's LHO is said to be in second seat.
See result merchant.
The player to the left of the player who has led to a trick.
A group of contestants in an event.
See Entry-shifting squeeze.
A ranking assigned to a contestant of relatively high rank.
A hand with 5–4–2–2 or 6–3–2–2 distribution.
A bid which is conditionally forcing: one which requests partner to rebid unless his hand is minimal or sub-minimal for his previous bidding.
A period of play during which those entered in an event play designated boards against designated opponents.
1) To defeat a contract.
In rubber bridge, an agreement that partners will not change at the end of each rubber.
Establish.
Semi-forcing.
(Of a call) A call that is not quite warranted by the strength of the hand making it.
1) (Verb) To lead a suit other than the one already played.
To try for an unusually good result by adopting an abnormal line of play, typically at matchpoint scoring.
The natural opening bid of 1♣ when the suit contains three cards or less.
1) In a 13-card hand, a singleton or void suit.
By agreement, a bid of a short side suit after a single raise, hoping to reach game.
In hand evaluation, points counted for singletons and voids.
Fail to follow suit.
To mix the cards. Shuffling seldom results in random distributions: in the long run, the cards so mixed rarely match the mathematical expectancies.
Partnership.
A secondary event played simultaneously with the main event.
A suit that is not trump; plain suit. A side suit may nevertheless have significant length: see Two-suiter.
The conventional meanings assigned to plays made by the defenders in order to exchange information.
1) A bid that requests that partner pass.

A sheet, typically of card or plastic, placed in the center of the table during the bidding period, and marked with numeral, suit and other symbols such that a player can indicate a call by tapping on them with a…
A squeeze against one opponent, in two suits, with the count (definition 3).
The normal manner of play, with certain knowledge only of one's own cards and dummy's, and without verbal communication between partners.
A round in a movement during which a pair is idle.
An irregular feature of a Mitchell movement: typically a move by the East–West pairs of 2 tables up instead of the usual 1, to avoid them playing the same boards twice.

entries.term: Skip bid warning, or skip-bid warning
entries.def: A warning to LHO that one is about to make a jump bid that could cause a revealing hesitation or huddle; used only when bidding screens are not in place.
A bid that invites partner to bid a slam.
(Slang) The location of a card that is onside.
Discard. Pronounced and sometimes spelled "sluff".
Cards that require establishment before they can be cashed.
A style of bidding that uses a jump to a contract (to which the previous bidding has already forced the partnership) to show a specific holding.
A rubber completed in three games. See fast rubber.
See discard. Neo-orthography for slough, as used in ruff and sluff.
(Slang) Same meaning as crack.

The Smith signal (also known as Smith echo or Smith peter) is an attitude carding signal in contract bridge showing additional values (or lack thereof) in the first suit led by the defence, while the signal itself is…
After opener has denied a four-card major in a Stayman sequence, responder's jump to 3M to show four cards in the bid major and five cards in the other major.
An endplay that captures an opponent's guarded trump by means of an overruff, when that card cannot be finessed in the normal fashion.
Lower honors, as distinct from aces and kings.
A suit strong enough to run without interruption, or (in the bidding) that requires no fit with partner.
To arrange one's cards by suit, and by rank within suit.
A conventional redouble that asks partner for rescue from a doubled contract.
A hand that is relatively strong for a call that is contemplated or that has been made.
A variant of Texas in which 4♣ and 4♦ are used as transfers to 4♥ and 4♠ respectively.
A singleton or void in a suit other than the trump suit.
An unusual jump bid that by agreement shows a fit for partner's last-bid suit and a singleton or void in the bid suit.
A menace in squeeze play which depends on values in both declarer's hand and dummy.

A position where the high cards of a tenace are in opposite hands, e.g. Ax opposite Qx; usually relevant only when a lead by an opponent with the missing honor card (here, the K) would be damaging to his side.
1) The organization that puts on a tournament, such as the WBF, the ACBL or the EBU, a regional association, or a club.
(Slang) Laydown.
A Suit Preference Signal, a card played by a defender to show interest in or an entry in a side suit.
A playing technique that forces the defender to discard a vital card, usually an apparent stopper.
A card whose lead forces one or both defenders to discard their guard in a suit.
A distribution of cards in defenders' hands that might make the play difficult for declarer.

entries.term: Standard American or Standard American Yellow Card (SAYC)
entries.def: A bidding system thought to conform to agreements that an unfamiliar partnership in America would use.
Not called to change seats during the movement being used.
A conventional bid of 2♣ that calls for a 1NT opening bidder to bid a four-card major, if one is held, and (usually) 2♦ otherwise.
To gain an advantage, usually through deception.
In the bidding, the space between one bid and the next highest. See Useful Space Principle.
A bid that conveys information on the basis of the number of steps it uses.
A squeeze that forces a defender either to be thrown in to act as a stepping-stone to a stranded dummy, or to allow declarer to establish a suit.
(Slang, chiefly British) An 1100-point penalty.
(Slang, adjective and noun) A singleton.
A bid that has no correlation to the bidder's hand, aiming to disturb conventions.
An instruction given to opponents when you make a jump bid, or skip bid.
1) To remove safe cards of exit from an opponent's hand.
A squeeze without the count in which one threat is against a safe exit card.
A double of a laydown contract made in hope of dissuading the opponents from successfully bidding to a higher, more rewarding contract.
A set of conventions that uses an opening bid of 1♣ as an artificial, forcing opening that promises a strong hand.
An opening notrump that shows a balanced hand and 15–17 or 16–18 HCP.
A bidding system that mandates a pass by first (or second) hand to show what other systems would regard as an opening bid.

entries.term: Strong two bid, strong two-bid, or Strong Two
entries.def: An agreement to use an opening bid of two of a suit to indicate a strong hand and a strong holding in the bid suit.
(Slang) Part-score.
(Slang) An ill-advised penalty double, such as one based on HCP when the bidding warns of freak distributions.
A ranked division of the deck of cards into (in descending rank order) spades, hearts, diamonds and clubs.
A defensive carding method that signals a preference, or the lack thereof, for a suit other than the suit used for the signal.
A strongly encouraging response to a transfer, such as a jump completion (e.g., 1NT – 2♥; 3♠).
A fit with partner's suit.
A double of an overcall that shows a fit for partner's suit, usually distinguished from a direct raise by the length of the suit in responder's hand.
A trick that in the absence of some irregularity a player must win, such as the ace of trumps.

A count or preference signal made in a different suit, usually the suit which declarer is running, to inform partner in beforehand about a critical decision he will have to make later during the play of the hand.
A deceptive bid or play.
A proxi-acronym for Sebesfi Woods 1NT Escape.
A difference in scores between two tables on a board in a team match.
An aggressive playing style, usually adopted by a pair or team who is behind with some chance to catch the leaders "with a little luck."
(Slang) Three consecutive passes, ending the auction.
A Swiss-system tournament for teams-of-four.
To lead a different suit.
see bidding system.
1) (Noun) A grouping of four players at a bridge tournament.
A large printed card placed on a table in a bridge tournament.
Awareness of opponents' behavior and mannerisms, leading to inferences regarding their holdings and problems on a deal.
1) Improper communication between partners, effected by words, gestures, or facial expressions.
(Slang) Huddle.
(Verb and noun) Slang. To adopt a line of defense that is intended to force declarer to ruff in the long hand.
1) (Adjective) (also Teams or Teams-of-four) A form of duplicate bridge played by eight people at two tables.
A member of the same team. Commonly said of any teammate other than one's partner.
(Adjective) See team (1)

1) Having the timing advantage in the play of the cards by possessing the lead and thereby being able to initiate (or continue) one's line of play before the declarer/opponents can establish his/theirs.
Waiting bid.
A convention whereby a bid of 4♦ or 4♥ in response to a notrump bid requests partner to transfer to 4♥ or 4♠ respectively.
A monthly magazine based in New York City, The Bridge World is the oldest continuously published periodical concerning contract bridge, and the game's most prestigious technical journal.
(Slang) A hand on which the opponents have the preponderance of strength.
(Slang) 1) A bid or contract based on less strength than normally recommended.

entries.term: Third from even, low from odd
entries.def: An opening lead convention that calls for the lead of the third-best card from a suit with an even number of cards, and the lowest card from a suit with an odd number of cards.
The player who makes the third call, or who is the third to play to a trick.
A term for a bidding system combining 5-card majors and a weak no-trump, such as the Kaplan–Sheinwold system.
An opening lead convention that calls for the lead of the third-best card in a suit of up to four card length, and the fifth-best in a longer suit.
In squeeze play, a menace.
A hand with length in three suits, thus shortness in the fourth.
To discard.
See Endplay.
(Slang) An honor card or honor sequence unaccompanied by low cards: "He had the ♥KQ tight."
A player's agenda for tasks in the play of the hand: for example, ruff losers and then draw trumps; or, draw trumps and then run the side suit.
The lead of a high spot card from a suit that contains no honor card.
A card that can take a trick on a given hand.
The sum of the number of tricks that each partnership can take, with its longest combined suit as trump.
Adjacent. Both cards and suits may be touching.
An organized duplicate bridge competition.
(Slang) Huddle.
In squeeze play, to shift the responsibility of controlling, or guarding, a menace from one opponent to the other.
Cards, such as aces and kings, that are valuable either in declarer's hands or in defenders'.
1) A pair or team whose members differ in "nationality".
See Sandbag.
A type of score slip on which the result of a deal is recorded for the purpose of comparative scoring.
See Board.

A natural bid that: (1) either shows a willingness to play in the denomination named, or promises or requests values in that denomination, and (2) by partnership agreement gives or requests additional information on…
A tripleton.
A (usually, high-level) tournament whose winners proceed to a subsequent event of even greater import.
See game try.
The score earned by contracting for and taking tricks.
A squeeze that is so-named because it consists of three simple squeezes against the same opponent.
A holding of three cards in a suit.
The ability, from a combination of the holding in trumps with play technique, to prevent the opponents from taking too many tricks in a plain suit.
An echo in the trump suit, long used to alert partner to the possibility of a defensive ruff, and in the early 21st century to give partner the count.

The advancement of a trump to the status of a winner by creating a position in which an opponent must suffer an uppercut, or an immediate adverse overruff, or choose to ruff with a higher trump that makes a later winner…
A squeeze that forces an opponent to weaken his holding in one of the threat suits enough that the suit can later be ruffed out.

By way of the auction, declarer and declarer's partner select the trump suit on the basis of their combined length and strength in the suit: the greater length to ruff more losers in the plain suits, and the greater…

entries.term: Two club system, or Two clubs system
entries.def: A bidding system that uses an opening bid of 2♣ as an artificial game force.

a bidding sequence in which after a one-level opening bid, there is a non-jump response at the two-level. a bidding system based upon the concept that after a one-level opening bid in a suit, a non-jump response by an…
A hand containing two long suits, usually each containing 4 or more cards, with at least 10 cards between the two suits.
An inquiry made after opener rebids 1NT.
An inquiry about the third (or sometimes fourth) position opener's strength in a major suit.
A finesse that could be taken through either opponent.
Over an opening bid of 1NT, the use of 2♣ as non-forcing Stayman and 2♦ as a game-forcing major suit inquiry.
Unauthorized information.
Information obtained from partner that one is not permitted to act on: for example, the manner in which partner plays a particular card, or the tone of voice when making a bid.
1) Broadly, any distribution of a hand or suit other than 4–3–3–3, 4–4–3–2 or 5–3–3–2.
A 13-card hand with unbalanced distribution in the broad or narrow sense just above.
A suit that has neither been bid nor indirectly shown.
See In front of.
1) (Verb) To bid less aggressively, or to a lower contract, than most would with the same cards.
To lead a low card when holding the top card or cards in a suit.
To play a trump lower than one already played on the lead of a plain suit.
A rubber that the players agree not to finish.
To discard lower cards that help prevent a higher card from being captured by an opponent.
See wide-ranging bid.
1) (Of a contract) Unable to be played so as to bring about a favorable outcome.
An artificial jump overcall in notrump that shows a Two-suiter, usually bid to suggest a sacrifice.

entries.term: Unusual over unusual (OUO), or Unusual vs. unusual
entries.def: A conventional method of conveying information after the opponents have deployed the unusual notrump convention or a Michaels Cue Bid, also called Unusual vs. Unusual.
Acronym or initialism for Unusual over unusual
To bid the lower of two adjacent suits before the higher.
Unpassed hand.
To ruff in the expectation of being overruffed, when the overruff will cause a trump in partner's hand to become a winner.
An agreement that when following suit to partner's lead, a low card encourages a continuation and a high card discourages.
United States Bridge Championships, competitions sponsored by the USBF in which entries compete to represent the United States in world tournaments.
United States Bridge Federation, the association charged with national representation of the United States in international competition.
A guide to developing bidding conventions and treatments that directs developers' attention to the allocation of bidding space.
A bidding system devised by Harold S. Vanderbilt and published by him in 1929, the first strong club system.
The use of a weak notrump when not vulnerable and a strong notrump when vulnerable.
Variable Cue Bidding. Agreements used in the Ultimate Club to request and show controls.
A conversion scale used in team contests and based on total IMP differences, so as to reduce the effect of very large swings.
The unblock of a winner opposite a threat prior to reaching a position that effects a squeeze.
A bidding system devised by Austrian player Paul Stern in the 1930s, in which an opening bid of 1NT is artificial and shows a strong hand.
An assumption about how the cards lie on a particular deal: "Sorry, partner, I took a view."
See Exclusion Blackwood.
A method of visually displaying tournament bridge deals to spectators, by optical or electronic means.
A bid that enables the bidder to obtain more information before making a commitment.
To condone an irregularity. In duplicate bridge, a waiver is an improper action.
(Slang) Push.
Duplicated values.
World Bridge Federation.
Welsh Bridge Union.
A jump overcall used to preempt the bidding.
A jump shift used to preempt the bidding.
A 1NT opening bid on a balanced hand with, usually, 12–14 HCP.

Following a major suit raise to the two level, the weak suit game try names a suit with at least three cards and at least two losers where partner's short suit is likely to be useful, as will a strong suit.

entries.term: Weak two bid, weak two-bid, or Weak Two
entries.def: An opening bid of two of a suit to indicate a relatively weak hand with a long suit.
A trick-taking card game and predecessor to contract bridge.
A count or total that obscures cards' identities.
(Said of a suit) Without a stopper.
A bid made within a wide range of strengths and shapes, the opposite of a limit bid.
A squeeze without the count that forces the defender to choose between a throw-in and an unblock, each of which is a losing option.
(Slang) Improper knowledge of a deal, prior to playing it.
After a jump rebid of 2NT by opener, responder's bid of 3♣ as a puppet to 3♦, after which responder can sign off with a weak hand.
A synonym or close variant of the Muiderberg convention, a weak two-bid showing 5 cards in a major and at least four cards in another suit.
The assignment of the numbers 4, 3, 2 and 1 as points to represent aces, kings, queens and jacks in the process of hand evaluation.
A card that is useful to a partnership, given the mesh of the cards in the two hands.
The world sport governing body for bridge.
(Verb) To place the contract in the less favorable hand for the partnership.
(lowercase) Any small card, of no trick-taking significance.
Cross-IMPs.
(uppercase) Redouble, in print or manuscript representation of the auction (where alternatives are "R", "Rdbl", etc.) or the final contract.
A convention to be used after a sequence like 1X – 1Y – 1NT.
A convention used in an uncontested auction where 3 suits are bid at the one level.
A convention to be used after a sequence like 1X – 1Y – (1Z) – 1NT, or 1X – (1Z) – 1Y – 1NT, where 1Z is an opponent's bid.
Originally, a hand with no card higher than a nine.
see Zoom.
An evaluation method to determine if a hand should be opened.
The lowest score obtained on a deal in a pairs game.
A specific type of falsecard which creates a losing option to declarer.
One of eight geographic zones in which World Bridge Federation member "nations" are grouped for some purposes.
In a relay system, the facility to joining into the next level of answers without needing to hear a new relay from partner.
